title: The Illusion of Perfect LLM Code
summary: I recently tested several different LLMs by tasking them with implementing a simple authentication feature for a web app. It is clear that almost all modern LLMs are now excellent at following a structured blueprint. However, the real differences appeared when looking under the hood at the security of the generated code.

title: One Decade Later: Revisiting Five Front-End Kung Fu Moves
summary: Exactly ten years ago, I gave a talk at the SF JavaScript meetup titled 5 Kung Fu Moves for Front-End Heroes. A full decade later, I found myself wondering: are these techniques still the secret sauce for modern front-end engineering, or have they been rendered obsolete by time?

title: Local, CPU-Friendly, High-Quality TTS (Text-to-Speech) with Kokoro
summary: Just a few years ago, realistic local speech generation seemed unimaginable. Today, its quality is exceptional and, crucially, it delivers these results without compromising privacy.

title: GTX 1080 Ti for Local LLM
summary: Despite being over eight years old, the NVIDIA GTX 1080 Ti remains a compelling choice for enthusiasts keen on running LLM locally.

title: Not Everything is an Agent
summary: “Agent” is likely going to be the word that will cause existential dread to true LLM enthusiasts.

title: Afterburner and Power Limit
summary: Ever witnessed a fighter jet spewing hot flames as it kicks into afterburner? In that moment, efficiency is deliberately sacrificed for maximum acceleration.

title: Privacy-Preserving Personal Search Appliance
summary: This is powered by SearXNG, an excellent open-source meta search engine.

title: LLM Inference Machine for $300
summary: You can absolutely run Qwen-2.5 32B. And of course, Llama-3.1 8B and Llama-3.2 Vision 11B are no problem at all.

title: Deploying an Uberjar to Dokku
summary: Dokku is a self-hosted Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) that offers a compelling alternative to popular PaaS solutions like Heroku. With built-in support for Linux containers, deploying an application on Dokku is straightforward. However, there is a lesser-known deployment method that involves sending a build artifact, such as a JAR package for Java apps, directly to Dokku.

title: Continuous Integration for React Native Apps with GitHub Actions
summary: For React Native mobile apps targeting Android and iOS, an easy way to setup its continuous integration is to take advantage of Actions, an automation workflow service provided by GitHub. Even better, for open-source projects, GitHub Action offers unlimited free running minutes (at the time of this writing).

title: On GitHub Actions with MSYS2
summary: Thanks to the complete GitHub Actions for MSYS2, it is easier than ever to construct a continuous integration setup for building with compilers and toolchains which can run on MSYS2.

title: Cross-compiling with musl Toolchains
summary: When working on command-line utilities which can be useful for various platforms, from Windows on x86 to Linux on MIPS, the existence of a cross-compilation is highly attractive. A number of different binaries can be constructed conveniently from a single, typically powerful host system.

title: Nix Package Manager on Ubuntu or Debian
summary: Even though Ubuntu/Debian is equipped with its legendary powerful package manager, dpkg, in some cases, it is still beneficial to take advantage of Nix, a purely functional package manager.

title: Practical Testing of Firebase Projects
summary: Your little Firebase project is getting bigger every day? Never underestimate the need to establish a solid and firm integration tests from the get go.

title: Search Box and Cloud Function
summary: For a blog hosted with Firebase Hosting, it turns out that a little search box is fairly easy to implement by using Cloud Functions for Firebase.

title: Automatic Merge of Pull Requests
summary: After using Azure DevOps for a while, I am totally sold on its Auto Complete feature for pull requests. While it does not apply universally, I do believe that any development process should be at the level where merging pull requests, or generalizing it, integrating all forms of contribution, should be as automatic and as hassle-free as possible.

title: Clang on Windows
summary: Thanks to the MSYS2 project, now there is an easy way to utilize Clang to build C/C++ application on Windows. This works equally well for both 32-bit and 64-bit programs.

title: Continuous Integration of Vanilla C Programs for Intel, ARM, and MIPS Architecture
summary: Developing cross-platform applications presents a major challenge:, how to ensure that every commit does not break some combinations of operating systems and CPU architectures. Fortunately, thanks an array of online services and open-source tools, this challenge becomes easier to tackle.

title: Cross Compiling with Docker on WSL 2
summary: Now that WSL 2 packs a true Linux kernel and supports Linux containers (via Docker), it can be a perfect setup to perform application cross compilations.

title: Basics of Memory Access in WebAssembly
summary: WebAssembly is getting more popular than ever. The use of WebAssembly outside the confinement of a web browser also starts to gain traction, as evidenced from projects such as WebAssembly Interface. Understanding the memory model of WebAssembly will be important in order to fully comprehend its power as well as its constraints.
